blocks.content: Grief doesn't leave on a schedule. It doesn't wait for you to be ready, doesn't give notice, doesn't check whether you have somewhere to be. It moves through a house the way weather does â€” present one morning and gone the next, then back again without warning in the middle of an otherwise ordinary Tuesday.
They called Dr. Reeves. She asked if they were still moving toward each other, and they said yes.
Daisy's book helped. The work required her to stay present.
Kit threw himself into a pitch — a short film about a boy and a field. He didn't tell Daisy what it was really about.

By December they had begun to laugh again. Not performed laughter â€” real laughter, the kind that surprised you. They cooked elaborate things on Sunday nights and watched old films on the couch with Daisy's legs across his lap and the city going dark outside the window.
